Journal.Title: IISE transactions on occupational ergonomics and human factors
Journal.ISOAbbreviation: IISE Trans Occup Ergon Hum Factors

Article.ArticleTitle: Predicting Behavioral Reliance on AI-Based Depression Treatment Recommendations Among Engineering Graduate Students: A Pilot Study Using EEG Signals.

Abstract.AbstractText: OCCUPATIONAL APPLICATIONSThis pilot study demonstrates the feasibility of using EEG-derived features to characterize behavioral reliance among engineering graduate students interacting with AI-labeled recommendations. Engineering professionals frequently engage with AI-supported decision systems in safety-critical and cognitively demanding contexts. In such occupational environments, inappropriate reliance, either over-reliance or unwarranted rejection, may compromise performance, safety, and decision quality. Although predictive performance was modest, the use of conservative participant-wise validation underscores the importance of rigorous evaluation when developing neurophysiological models for occupational human-AI interaction. These findings support EEG as a complementary tool for studying reliance behavior in professional settings while highlighting the need for multimodal and context-sensitive approaches in real-world engineering applications.

AffiliationInfo.Affiliation: Armstrong Institute Center for Health Care Human Factors, Johns Hopkins Medicine, Johns Hopkins University, Baltimore, MD, USA.
Department of Industrial and Management Systems Engineering, Benjamin M. Statler College of Engineering and Mineral Resources, West Virginia University, Morgantown, WV, USA.

MedlineJournalInfo.MedlineTA: IISE Trans Occup Ergon Hum Factors

OtherAbstract.AbstractText: Objectives: This study investigated whether EEG signals could predict behavioral reliance of engineering graduate students on AI-labeled treatment recommendations during depression-related decision-making, using conservative participant-wise machine-learning evaluation.Methods: Thirteen participants completed ten depression treatment scenarios in a task-based Qualtrics experiment. Each scenario presented three treatment options; one labeled as the AI recommendation. The AI-labeled option was clinically appropriate in five scenarios and inappropriate in another five scenarios. Behavioral reliance was operationalized as selection versus non-selection of the AI-labeled option. EEG signals were recorded from frontal electrodes (F3, Fz, F4, FC1, FC2). Beta-band (13–30 Hz) power features were extracted from 5-s EEG segments ending 1 s before decision. Random Forest (RF) and Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGB) classifiers were evaluated using a leave-one-participant-out (LOPO) framework, with nested 5-fold cross-validation applied within training folds for hyperparameter tuning.Results: Frontal beta activity was significantly higher during task engagement compared to resting baseline, reflecting increased cognitive effort during decision-making; however, no significant differences were observed across individual scenarios. Under participant-wise LOPO evaluation, machine-learning models demonstrated modest, above-chance discrimination of behavioral reliance. XGB achieved AUROC of 0.636, with wide variability across held-out participants.Conclusion: Short EEG segments contained limited but detectable information related to behavioral reliance on AI-labeled recommendations. However, modest performance and strong inter-individual variability indicate that EEG alone is insufficient for robust reliance prediction. These findings underscore the importance of conservative validation strategies and a clear distinction between behavioral reliance and subjective trust in human–AI interaction research.
